region_of_origin: West Africa / Ethiopia (origin); widely cultivated across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Caribbean; common in Singapore Indian, Malay, and Chinese cooking
food_subgroup: Fresh vegetable — tender green fruiting pods with characteristic mucilaginous interior; consumed whole or sliced, raw or cooked

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for fresh raw okra pods. Closest parent is FOODON_03301710 (okra, whole or parts). The specific preparation state (fresh, raw, 5 pods) is not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of fresh raw okra is approximately 5 medium pods, weighing about 100g, as commonly sold at Singapore wet markets.

nutrient_source.title: Okra, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169228); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169228 is a direct match for raw okra. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for regional validation. All per-100g values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of okra — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature; GI ~20 for raw okra
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore dietary guidance
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of approximately 20 for okra is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for non-starchy green vegetables and is widely cited in peer-reviewed literature for Abelmoschus esculentus. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (20 × 7.5) / 100 = 1.5 per 100g serving. Confidence 0.9 — referenced value, not a direct single-study measurement from the Sydney database for this exact preparation.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7mg/100g), high potassium (299mg/100g) and magnesium (57mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Okra is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients: its very low sodium content (7mg/100g), high potassium (299mg/100g), and significant magnesium (57mg/100g) collectively support blood pressure reduction via the DASH dietary pattern; calcium (82mg/100g) provides additional cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~20), GL ~1.5 per 100g serving; viscous mucilaginous fibre (pectin and beta-glucan-like polysaccharides) may attenuate postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Okra is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management: with a GI of approximately 20 and GL of 1.5, it is essentially a free food glycaemically; its characteristic mucilaginous fibre (rhamnogalacturonans, arabinogalactans) forms a viscous intestinal gel that slows glucose absorption — small controlled crossover studies suggest 15–30% reduction in postprandial glucose, though large RCT evidence is still pending; recommend freely as a low-cost, widely available T2DM-friendly vegetable at Singapore wet markets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (3.2g/100g), high folate (88mcg/100g), and significant potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Okra is cardioprotective: it contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, provides 3.2g dietary fibre per 100g (supporting LDL reduction), and its high folate (88mcg/100g) helps manage homocysteine levels; the mucilaginous polysaccharides may also bind bile acids in the gut, contributing to cholesterol reduction analogous to soluble fibre mechanisms.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (88mcg/100g) critical for patients on methotrexate; magnesium and calcium support bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Okra is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients: its high folate content (88mcg/100g) is clinically significant for patients on methotrexate (which depletes folate); magnesium (57mg/100g) supports anti-inflammatory enzyme pathways; calcium (82mg/100g) benefits bone health in patients on glucocorticoids; note that okra contains vitamin K (~31mcg/100g) which should be considered for patients on warfarin anticoagulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lady's Finger / Okra (秋葵 / Bendi / வெண்டைக்காய் / भिंडी — Abelmoschus esculentus) is a tender green fruiting pod vegetable widely consumed across Singapore's Indian, Malay, and Chinese communities. Fresh raw okra has a mild, slightly grassy flavour with a distinctive mucilaginous (slimy) texture when cut, produced by viscous polysaccharides including pectin and beta-glucan-like rhamnogalacturonans and arabinogalactans. This mucilage is the defining clinical feature of okra: it forms a viscous gel in the small intestine that slows carbohydrate digestion and glucose absorption, a mechanism analogous to oat beta-glucan. Small controlled crossover studies suggest 15–30% reduction in postprandial glucose with regular okra consumption, making it one of very few common vegetables with a plausible mechanistic basis for glucose attenuation beyond simply being low-GI. Nutritionally, per 100g raw okra provides: 33 kcal, 1.9g protein, 0.2g fat, 7.5g carbohydrate, 3.2g dietary fibre, 7mg sodium, 299mg potassium, 82mg calcium, 57mg magnesium, 88mcg folate, 23mg vitamin C, and 36mcg vitamin A. GI is approximately 20 (very low) with a glycaemic load of only 1.5 per 100g serving — essentially a free food for diabetes management. Okra is a staple at Singapore wet markets, affordable and available year-round, making it an important dietary recommendation for lower-income patients with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. Common preparations include Singapore Indian stir-fry (with mustard seeds and curry leaves), Malay sambal bendi, and Chinese stir-fry with garlic. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets.
meal_context: Singapore Indian stir-fry with mustard seeds and curry leaves

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169228 (raw okra, exact match) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. All core macros and secondary nutrients are confirmed from approved primary sources. GI value of approximately 20 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for non-starchy green vegetables and widely cited in peer-reviewed literature for Abelmoschus esculentus.
notes: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169228 — raw okra contains negligible saturated fatty acids (reported as 0.026g/100g, rounded to 0.0g). trans_fat_g is confirmed zero — naturally zero in fresh raw vegetables. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — naturally zero in raw okra per USDA SR Legacy. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.03g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.03g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169228. water_g (89.6g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169228. Vitamin K content of approximately 31mcg/100g (not included in per_100g schema) is clinically relevant for patients on warfarin — noted in rheumatoid arthritis health context. GI confidence is 0.9 (referenced/analogous estimate) rather than 1.0 as no single direct Sydney GI Database study for this exact preparation was identified; value is consistent across multiple peer-reviewed sources. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is the closest parent (okra whole or parts); FoodEx2 code A00LC (Okra) is an exact match; SNOMED CT 256329008 (Okra substance) is an exact match.
